title: At the Airport
description: Learn essential vocabulary and phrases for navigating airports confidently.

summary.grammar: Use 'going to' for planned travel and 'will' for spontaneous decisions at the airport. Example: 'I'm going to check in now.' vs 'I'll carry that for you.'

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Excuse me, where is the check-in counter for Air France?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It's on the second floor, counter number 14 to 18.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Thank you! And do you know if my flight is on time?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: You can check the departure board right there. It shows real-time updates.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Oh perfect. My boarding pass says Gate C7. Is that far?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It's about a 10-minute walk. You should head there soon — boarding starts in 30 minutes.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Great, thank you so much for your help!

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Have a safe flight!

quiz.question: Where do you go to get your ticket stamped before the flight?
Which phrase uses 'going to' correctly?
options: I'm going to board the plane.

title: Job Interviews
description: Master professional vocabulary and confident communication for job interviews.

summary.grammar: Use Present Perfect to talk about experience: 'I have worked in customer service for 3 years.' Use 'would' for hypothetical questions: 'I would handle the situation by communicating clearly.'

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Good morning, please have a seat. Can you tell me a little about yourself?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Of course! I'm a marketing graduate with two years of experience in digital campaigns.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: That's great. What would you say is your biggest strength?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I'd say my ability to manage multiple projects under tight deadlines is my strongest point.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: And how do you handle working under pressure?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I prioritize tasks, break them into smaller steps, and stay focused on the outcome.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Excellent. Do you have any questions for us?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yes — could you tell me more about the team I'd be working with?

quiz.question: What document summarizes your work history?
Which tense do we use to talk about past experience?

title: At the Doctor
description: Learn medical vocabulary and how to describe symptoms and health concerns clearly.

summary.grammar: Use 'have been + -ing' for ongoing symptoms: 'I have been feeling dizzy for two days.' Use 'since' and 'for' to describe duration.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Good afternoon. What seems to be the problem today?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I've been having a terrible headache and a sore throat for the past three days.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: I see. Do you have any fever or difficulty breathing?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I had a slight fever last night, but it went down this morning.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Let me check your temperature and blood pressure. Are you allergic to any medication?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yes, I'm allergic to penicillin. It gives me a rash.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Noted. I'll prescribe something safe. Take this twice a day after meals for 5 days.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Should I come back if I don't feel better?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Yes, schedule a follow-up in a week if symptoms persist.

quiz.question: What do we call a physical reaction to medication?
Which sentence is correct?
options: I have been feeling sick since Monday.
I was feeling sick since Monday.
I am feeling sick for two days.

title: Shopping & Bargaining
description: Navigate shopping situations, compare prices, and negotiate confidently in English.

summary.grammar: Use comparative adjectives for shopping: 'This one is cheaper than that one.' Use 'too' and 'enough': 'It's too expensive.' / 'It's affordable enough.'

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Hi, I'm looking for a pair of running shoes. Do you have anything in size 42?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yes, we have a few options. This pair is our bestseller — it's lightweight and very comfortable.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: It looks nice! How much does it cost?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It's originally $120, but it's currently 20% off, so it comes to $96.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Can you go a little lower? I'd take it for $85.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I'm afraid I can't go below $90. That's the best I can do.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Alright, deal! Can I pay by card?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Absolutely. I'll get you a receipt and a bag.

quiz.question: Which comparison is correct?
options: This is more cheap than that.
This is the cheap of all.

quiz.question: What do you receive as proof of purchase?

title: Making Plans
description: Learn how to make, accept, and politely decline plans and invitations in English.

summary.grammar: Use 'shall', 'how about', and 'why don't we' for suggestions. Use Present Continuous for future plans: 'I'm meeting Sarah on Friday.'

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Hey! Are you free this Saturday evening?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I think so. What did you have in mind?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Some friends and I are going to a rooftop restaurant downtown. Would you like to join us?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: That sounds lovely! What time are you planning to meet?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: We're thinking around 7:30. Is that convenient for you?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: 7:30 works perfectly. Shall I make a reservation, or have you already sorted that?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: It's all sorted! Just let me know if anything comes up and you can't make it.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: I will. I'm really looking forward to it!

quiz.question: Which sentence correctly uses Present Continuous for future plans?
How do you politely decline a plan?
options: I'm afraid I can't make it — I have a prior commitment.

title: Renting an Apartment
description: Understand rental agreements, landlord-tenant communication, and housing vocabulary.

summary.grammar: Use modal verbs for rights and obligations: 'You must pay rent by the 1st.' / 'You can't sublet without permission.' / 'The landlord should fix the heating.'

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Hello, I'm calling about the two-bedroom apartment listed online. Is it still available?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Yes, it is! Would you like to schedule a viewing?

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Absolutely. Can you tell me a bit more about it first? Is it furnished?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It comes partially furnished — there's a bed, sofa, and kitchen appliances.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: What about utilities? Are they included in the rent?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Water and internet are included. Electricity is paid separately by the tenant.

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: And what's the security deposit?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: It's two months' rent, refundable at the end of your lease if there's no damage.

quiz.question: What is a 'security deposit'?
options: A refundable payment made before moving in
